response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":33,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"cleaner","city_of_living":"Lecce","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["running","cycling","board games"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":4,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":20,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":30,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":49,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":32,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"It feels like these social media feeds, when I do check them, are just a mess of things that aren't true. When I’m out running or playing a board game, my mind is quiet, but when I look at my phone, it’s all this noise, and sometimes that noise just makes me feel heavier, like when the sadness settles in. Dealing with all this fake stuff is tiring, honestly, and I think the first thing you have to do is protect your own peace. Because, you know, when you’re already feeling a bit low, seeing some wild, untrue story can just make that feeling bigger. I don't have a lot of energy to fight every lie out there, so the best management strategy for me is really about setting boundaries. I try to remember that what I see online isn't real life; it’s just someone trying to look good or push an idea, and that doesn't mean it's fact. Instead of trying to fact-check everything, which is exhausting and frankly, not my strength, I try to be much more selective about who I even listen to. If a page or a person always seems to be shouting something dramatic or something that just feels wrong in my gut, I mute them or unfollow them. It’s like cleaning a room; if there are too many things that are dirty or messy, you just have to take some things out of the room so you can breathe. For me, that means focusing on the small, reliable things—like the routine of my cycle rides or the simple rules of a good game—because those things are real. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It feels like these social media feeds, when I do check them, are just a mess of things that aren't true. When I’m out running or playing a board game, my mind is quiet, but when I look at my phone, it’s all this noise, and sometimes that noise just makes me feel heavier, like when the sadness settles in. Dealing with all this fake stuff is tiring, honestly, and I think the first thing you have to do is protect your own peace. Because, you know, when you’re already feeling a bit low, seeing some wild, untrue story can just make that feeling bigger. I don't have a lot of energy to fight every lie out there, so the best management strategy for me is really about setting boundaries. I try to remember that what I see online isn't real life; it’s just someone trying to look good or push an idea, and that doesn't mean it's fact. Instead of trying to fact-check everything, which is exhausting and frankly, not my strength, I try to be much more selective about who I even listen to. If a page or a person always seems to be shouting something dramatic or something that just feels wrong in my gut, I mute them or unfollow them. It’s like cleaning a room; if there are too many things that are dirty or messy, you just have to take some things out of the room so you can breathe. For me, that means focusing on the small, reliable things—like the routine of my cycle rides or the simple rules of a good game—because those things are real. Don't let the endless, shaky stories online steal the quiet moments that keep you steady.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is depressed, has low conscientiousness and extraversion, and low openness, suggesting a preference for routine, low energy, and a need for emotional safety. The advice must be low-effort, boundary-focused, and centered on protecting inner peace rather than engaging in rigorous fact-checking. The tone should be quiet, somewhat weary, and reflective.
